text: AVIANO, a town of Italy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. of Udine, on the r. bank of the Livenza, 38 m. W of Udine, and 9 NNW of Pordenone. Pop. 5,000.
AVICH (LOCH), a small sheet of water, about 6 m. in circumf., in the district of Lorn, in Argyleshire, 2 m. distant from the NE extremity of Loch-Awe.
AVIEMORE, a village in Morayshire, 13 m. NE of Pitmain, on the W bank of the Spey, at the base of Craigellachie.

AVIGLIANA, or AVILLANO, a commune and town of Italy, in Piedmont, in the prov. and 15 m. ESE of Susa. It occupies a fine situation on a low offset of the Cottian Alps, and possesses the advantages of a salubrious climate and fertile soil. The town contains 2 parish churches and a strong castle; and has some manufactories of common cloth, and silk spinning-mills. Pop. 3,000.

AVIGLIANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicato, in the district and 10 m. NNW of Potenza. It stands on the slope of a hill, and contains a fine collegiate church, several convents, and a royal college. The cattle of this locality are reputed to be the finest in the kingdom.
AVIGNANO, a town of Naples, in the Terra-di- Lavoro, 12 m. NE of Capua.

AVIGNON, a town of France, the cap. of the dep. and the arrond. of Vaucluse, situated on the l. bank of the Rhone, about 3 m. above the influx of the Durance, 25 m. ENE from Nîmes, and 477 m. SSE from Paris; in N lat. 42° 57′ 8″, E long. 4° 48′ 15″. This ancient city is seated in a fertile plain, at an alt. of 90 ft. above sea-level. Mount Ventoux rises to the N of A., its sides glowing with all the varied hues of vegetation, while its summit is veiled in snow; on the S the horizon is bounded by the chain of the Angles and the Issarts. The plains of Languedoc, rich in mulberry and olive trees, and sprinkled with undulating hills covered with vineyards, look like a vast garden spread over the country; to the E are seen the abrupt and sterile rocks of Vaucluse. It forms a nearly regular oval; and is enclosed by lofty embattled ramparts, flanked with watch-towers. The boulevards, of equal extent with the walls, form delightful promenades. The houses, which are of stone, are wellbuilt; but the streets generally are narrow, angular, and irregular; and Inglis says, "its filthiness is disgusting, absolutely inconceivable." The streets are so narrow as to admit of being covered with awnings of canvass in the summer, a custom which prevails in all the towns of Provence. [Millin.] Many relics of the former ecclesiastical importance of this town still exist. The principal of these is the metropolitan church, an edifice of great antiquity, rebuilt by Charlemagne, and embellished by successive pontiffs. In 1680 Archbishop Libelli annexed to this church the chapel of the Resurrection, a chef d'œuvre of sculpture. The church of St. Pierre, rebuilt in 1358, and that of St. Agricol are both interesting structures. Of the church of the Cordeliers, enclosing the tombs of 'the brave Crillon,' and the beautiful Laura, immortalized by the muse of Petrarch, only the belfry remains. The papal palace,—an irregular structure, with massive walls, subterranean passages, and concealed entrances,-forms a perfect specimen of the military architecture of the period. Part of this building is now converted into a barrack; the rest is used as a prison. The mint, built under the auspices of pope Paul V., is now converted into a guard-house. The town-hall is surmounted with a curiously constructed belfry. Of modern institutions, the most important are the public library, containing 45,000 vols., and numerous

MSS., a museum of arts and sciences, the theatre, an ecclesiastical seminary, a literary society called the academy of Vaucluse, a royal college, a normal school, and the botanic garden. The Rhone flows on the W side of the city, and is crossed by a wooden structure built on the ruins of a magnificent stonebridge which was destroyed by an inundation in 1669. This bridge connects the city with a town of 3,000 inhabitants, known as Villeneuve-les-Avignon. The quays, which extend along the banks of the river, are magnificent.—The principal manufactures of A. are silk-fabrics, velvets, handkerchiefs, and musical instrument strings. It also possesses iron, copper, tin, and cannon foundries, which have been established in the church of St. Dominic, formerly that of the Inquisition; also dye works, silk and cotton spinning-mills, and several letter-press printing establishments which during the papal government used to be chiefly employed in printing pirated editions of copyright works. The commerce of A. has AVI been nearly all transferred to Lyons; and Beaucaire has usurped its market; what remains consists in grain, pulse, madder, wine, brandy, oil, colonial produce, raw silk, tanned skins, horses, mules, and cattle. A. must be regarded as a decayed city, for within the circuit of its walls large spaces once covered with buildings are now vacant; its theatres have been converted into warehouses, its palaces into barracks; and the pop., which in Louis XIV.'s time amounted to 80,000, was, in 1836, only 31,786; and in 1846, only 31,029. Before the Revolution, A. was a city of priests, containing 8 chapters, 35 convents, 10 hospitals, 7 penitent fraternities, 3 seminaries, and 60 churches.—The climate of A. is distinguished by rapid and irregular transitions of temp. Parching summers are often succeeded by severely cold winters. The yearly fall of rain is estimated at 18.19 inches. [Fischer.]—This city lays claim to great antiquity. Before the Roman supremacy it was the cap. of a Gaulish tribe, by whom it was called Avenio Cavarum. After many vicissitudes, it fell by succession to Philipe le Bel, who conferred it in 1290 upon Charles, king of Sicily. From 1307 to 1377 it was a papal residence. Clement VI., in 1348, purchased the city from the queen of Sicily for 80,000 gold crowns, which were never paid. The popes governed the city and its dependent county by a cardinal legate; but the inhabitants were considered as naturalized in France. Of course it formed a place of refuge to a multitude of delinquents, who fled hither to escape their creditors or the arm of justice. The sovereignty was retained by the popes till 1791, when the papal legate was expelled, and the town was reunited to France, after being desolated by the demoniac excesses of revolutionary fanaticism. It is now the seat of an archb., metropolitan of Nimes, Valence, Viviers, and Montpellier, and whose dio. comprehends the dep. of Vaucluse. The Avignose are said to be remarkable for their vivacity; and the women for their beauty. A. was the birth-place of Berton de Crillon, Folard, F. d'Urban, and Joseph Vernet.—The arrond. of A., comprising 49,732 hectares, divided into the 5 cantons of Avignon N and S, Bedarrides, Cavaillon, and L'Isle, and subdivided into 21 com., had a pop. in 1836 of 69,820; in 1846, of 76,483.—Tableau d'Avignon, 1838.—Chaillot, Histoire d'Avignon, 3 vols. 8vo., 1818.—Inglis.—Fischer. —Countess of Blessington's 'Idler in Italy.'
AVIGNONET, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, in the arrond. and canton of and 4 m. ESE of Villefranche-de-Lauraguais. Pop. 2,267.
AVIGUAL, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, at the junction of the river Remedio or Verde, with the San Francisco.

A'VILA, a province of Spain, in the kingdom of Old Castile, bounded on the N by Valladolid; on the E by Segovia; on the S by Toledo; and on the W by Salamanca. It lies between 40° 5' and 41° 13' N lat., and 4° 15' and 5° 30' W long.; and comprises a superficial area of 2,600 sq. m. This prov., the most elevated of the central districts of the peninsula, is intersected by numerous mountain-ridges, of which the principal are those of Quadarrama to the SE, and the Sierra D'Avila, a continuation of the same chain. In the higher regions, the temperature is of course cold; in the lower, mild during winter, and in summer frequently oppressively warm. The valleys, especially those of Alberche and la Paramira D'Avila, are rich and luxuriant. Several small rivers take their rise in this prov. Of these the most important are the Alberche and the Tietar, in the S; and the Adaja, Eresma, Zapardiel, and Tormes, which flow N, and fall into the Duero. The lakes are also inconsiderable. The largest is that of Gredos. There are several mineral springs. The soil is to a great extent calcareous and stony. Manorial and feudal rights are very oppressive, and agriculture is much neglected; the cereal productions of the province are, however, adequate to the consumption. Lint, pimento, olives, the mulberry, and the vine, are also successfully cultivated. Various kinds of nuts are indigenous. Wood is generally scarce. The rearing of silk-worms forms an important branch of industry. Numerous flocks,—the wool of which is of fine quality,—find pasture in the rich herbage of this country. The principal manufactures are fine cloth, linen, paper, and earthenware. There are also tanneries and forges. Its chief mineral productions are iron and coal; veins of silver have also been discovered; but owing to the natural indolence of the Spanish character, the mining operations of the prov. have hitherto been conducted on a small scale. The commerce chiefly consists in cloth, pimento, and iron. The pop. in 1803 was estimated at 118,061; in 1834, at 137,903. — It is divided into 6 partidos: viz., Arenas-de-San-Pedro, Arevalo, Avila, Barco-de- Avila, Cebreros, and Piédrahita; which are subdivided into 389 pueblos or communes.

AVILA, the cap. of the above prov., is situated on the r. bank of the Adaja, about 60 m. WNW from Madrid, on an elevated plain, at an alt. of 3,480 ft. above sea-level. This once rich and flourishing city is surrounded by ancient walls, and retains many monuments of its former importance. The streets are spacious, and built with great regularity. There are 9 parish-churches, numerous convents, and several hospitals. Since 1811 the university has been suppressed. The only local manufactures are woollen fabrics, hats, and cotton-prints. A. is the seat of a bishop, suffragan of the archb. of Santiago, and is celebrated as the birth-place, in 1515, of Maria Theresa—"Nuestra Serafica Madre" -whose spiritual writings are highly esteemed in Spain. This town had 1,234 houses and 4,121 inhabitants in 1844.

AVILA, a mountain-chain of Spain, in the prov. of the same name. It forms a curve from W to E; and unites with the Sierra-Malagon to the E of the town of Avila. The principal rivers which take their rise in this range are the Adaja and the Alberche.
AVILA, a town of Ecuador, in the district of Pichincha, on the r. side of the Suno or Avila, and about 40 m. NNW of the confluence of that river with the Napo.

AVILES (SAN NICOLAS DE), a town of Spain, in the prov. of Asturias, near the mouth of the river of the same name, 15 m. N of Oviedo. It contains 995 houses, with a pop. of 4,115; and has 2 parishchurches, 2 convents, and an hospital. The approach to the town is defended by Fort St. Jean, and the river is here crossed by a fine stone-bridge. The principal manufactures are cloth and copper-ware. There is one annual fair. The port admits only small vessels.
AVILES, a town of Guatemala, in the prov. of Nicaragua, at the junction of the Bernando with the gulf of Conchagua; 50 m. NNW of Leon.

AVIN, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 5 m. SE of Huy. It is memorable for the victory of the French over the Spaniards in 1635.—Also a v. of Persia, in Azerdbijan, at the base of the Elburz, celebrated for its beautiful gardens. Vigne saw a chenar or Oriental plane-tree here, the trunk of which measured 64 ft. in circumf.
AVINGTON, a parish in Berkshire, 2 m. E by S of Hungerford. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 93.—Also a parish in Southamptonshire, 4 m. NE of Winchester. Area 1,830 acres. Pop. 204.
AVINOFF (CAPE), a promontory of Russian America, on Behring's sea, in N lat. 60°, W long. 164°. AVL
AVIO, a town of Tyrol, 12 m. SSW of Roveredo, on the r. bank of the Adige, 470 ft. above sea-level. It has some manufactures of silk and velvet, and a flint-quarry. Pop. 3,200.
AVION, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pasde-Calais, cant. of Vimy. Pop. 1,139.
AVIOTH, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Montmedy. Pop. 434.
AVIREY-LINGEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aube, cant. of Les Riceys. Pop. 895.
AVISFORD, a hundred in the rape of Arundel, Sussex, containing 12 parishes. Area 15,410 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,976; in 1841, 2,877.
AVIT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Pontaumur. Pop. 863.
AVIT-DU-MOIRON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant of St. Foy. Pop. 1,019.
AVIT-SENIEUR (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Beaumont. Pop. 1,207.

AVIZ, a river of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, and comarca of Aviz, which takes its rise in the Sierra-Alpedreira, and running in a WNW direction, unites with the Ervedal about 15 m. NE of the town of Aviz.-Also a comarca and town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, on the r. bank of the Ervedal, 5 m. above its confluence with the Anhaloura or Zatas, and 44 m. WSW of Portalegre. It contains a parish-church, a convent, an hospital, and a castle; and was the head-quarters of the knights of the order of Aviz, founded in 1146 by Alphonso I. Pop. 1,500.

AVIZE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, and arrond. of Epernay.-The cant. comprises 19 communes, and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 8,269.—The town is 6 m. SE of Epernay, and is the principal entrepot of the wines of Champagne, which are kept in immense vaults excavated in the solid rock. The environs afford excellent white wines. Pop. 1,495.

AVLA, a town of Greece, in the Morea, and nomarchy of Achaia, near the l. bank of the Kamenitza, and 8 m. ESE of the town of that name. AVLO'NA, AULONA, or VALONA, a sanjak or pashalik of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Albania, bounded on the N by the sanjaks of Elbassan and Scutari; on the E by those of Ochrida and Bitolia; on the S by Janina; on the SW by Delvino; and on the W by the Ionian sea. It stretches from 39° 48' to 41° 2' N lat., forming the whole southern part of Albania, from the Uskomobin on the N, to the Khimera range on the S, and presents a total superficies of 1,350 sq. m. It is generally mountainous, with a few narrow fertile valleys; and is watered by the Samana, the Tuberathi or Ergent, and the Vojutza or Poro, and their numerous tributaries. The principal productions of its soil are tobacco, and several varieties of fruit, particularly olives, cotton, honey, and wax. The rearing of cattle forms an important branch of its rural industry. The forests are large, and yield excellent pine-timber, and large quantities of Valonia acorns. Petroleum is extensively obtained at Salenitza.

AVLO'NA, the ancient Aulon of the Greeks, the cap. of the above sanjak, is situated on the gulf of the same name, in N lat. 40° 28', E long. 19° 27', 25 m. SW of Berat. It is about half-an-hour's walk from the shore, and is defended by a citadel. It possesses a good port, protected by the island Sasseno, the ancient Saso, and one of the safest on this coast, but the anchorage is rocky. The surrounding marshes and rice-grounds render the atmosphere so insalubrious that during the hot season the town is almost completely deserted. The environs present numerous olive-plantations, intermingled with villas COLORIO CONTROL COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORIO COLORI and tombs. The pop., including that of the suburbs, may amount to 8,000, Mahommedans, Christians, and Jews; the latter immigrated to this place from Ancona, whence they were banished under the pontificate of Paul IV. The Christians are chiefly engaged in commercial pursuits; the Turks in manufacturing woollen stuffs and arms. Considerable quantities of salt, earth-oil or petroleum, olive-oil, and valonia are exported. The entrance to the gulf of A. is 6 leagues to the S of the Samane river. The height of the surrounding mountains gives it the appearance of a perfect basin.

AVNIG, a fort of Turkey in Asia, in the pashalik of Kars, and district of Pasin, situated to the N of the Aras, and to the E of Medjengherd. This fort was repeatedly besieged and taken by Tamerlane and his successors. It is supposed to be the Castrum Abnicum of Constantine Porphyrogénitus.
AVOCH, a parish in Ross-shire, bounded on the SE by the Moray frith. Pop. in 1831, 1,956; in 1841, 1,931, of whom 742 were in the v. of A., or Seatown of A., which is chiefly inhabited by fishers.
AVOCOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Varennes. It has manufactories of earthenware and nails. Pop. 944.

AVOISE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Sable, at the confluence of the Deux Ponts with the Sarthe, 14 m. NNW of La Fleche. It has a paper-mill, and considerable commerce in iron, slate, and timber. Pop. 1,174.

AVOLA, or AULA, a town of Sicily, in the district and 5 m. ENE of Nota, and 14 m. SW of Syracuse, on the Mediterranean. It is a modern built town, with regular streets and low houses; and has a small port. It has a sugar-refinery and manufactories of cordage; and conducts a considerable commerce in wine, grain, cheese, oil, honey, sugar, almonds, and cattle. In the environs are the only now existing sugar-plantations in the island. In the vicinity is the subterranean passage of the Cassibili. The present town occupies the site of the ancient Aula, which was destroyed in 1693. Pop. 7,000.

AVOLD (SAINT), or SANTAFOR, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Moselle, and arrond. of Sarreguemines.—The cant. comprises 18 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 15,044. The town lies 20 m. W of Sarreguemines, and 25 m. E of Metz. It has manufactories of Prussian blue, tanneries, and dye-works. In the vicinity is a mineral spring. Pop. 3,451.

AVON, a river which rises near Devizes, flows past Salisbury, and falls into the English channel a little below Christchurch, after a course of 65 m.— Also a river in Glamorganshire, falling into the E side of Swansea bay, after a course of 16 m.—Also a stream in Monmouthshire, joining the Usk at Caerleon.—Also a stream falling into Cardigan bay, near Barmouth.—Also a river which rises near Naseby in Northamptonshire, and after passing Stratford-on-Avon and Evesham, joins the Severn at Tewkesbury, after a course of about 100 m. It is navigable by barges of 40 tons to Stratford. Its tributaries are the Alne, the Leame, the Stour, the Sow, and the Swift. It is sometimes called the Upper A.—Also a river rising near Tetbury in Gloucestershire, passing Bath and Bristol, and falling into the Bristol channel 10 m. below the latter city, after a course of 80 m. It is connected with the Thames by the Kennet and Avon canal. -Also a tributary of the Annan, which it joins below Moffat.—Also a stream in Lanarkshire, which, after a course of 28 m., joins the Clyde 1 m. to the SE of the town of Hamilton.—Also a river of New South Wales, which takes its rise in Brown's range, in the co. of Durham, crosses the church and school REPARE CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRA AVR estate, and entering the co. of Gloucester curves to the N, and unites with the Gloucester river in S lat. 32°, and E long 151° 57'.—Also a river of Nova Scotia, which falls into the basin of Minas, formed by three head-branches, the Windsor or Avon, the St. Croix, and the Kennetcoot. See AVEN.

AVON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Fontainebleau. Pop. 1,227. -Also a township of Maine, U. S., in Franklin co. Pop. 827.—Also a township of Connecticut, U. S., in Hartford-co., 10 m. WNW of Hartford. The district watered by the Farmington possesses considerable fertility. Pop. 1,001.—Also a post village of New York, U. S., in Livingston co., 222 m. NW of Albany. It is pleasantly situated on a rising ground, about half a mile E of the Genesee river. To the SW of the village are two celebrated mineral springs and baths. Pop. 2,999.—Also a township of Ohio, U.S., in Lorain co., 17 m. W of Cleveland, and 135 m. NNE of Columbus. It is situated on Lake Erie, and is intersected by French Creek. Pop. 1,220.-- Also a township of Michigan, U. S., in Oatland co. Pop. 1,630.
AVON ISLANDS, two islands and a chain of reefs near Torres straits, in about S lat. 19° 30', E long. 158° 10'.
AVONBEG, one of the three head-streams of the Ovoca in co. Wicklow. It comes from Lake Finogefin, and joins the Avonmore, after a SE course of 13 m.

AVONDALE, a parish in Lanarkshire, a great part of which forms the strath or valley of the Avon river,-"a fertile region green with wood" [Wordsworth]. Its superficial area is about 40,000 acres. Pop. in 1831, 5,761; in 1841, 6,180, of whom 3,852 were in the town of Strathaven.
AVONGO, a river of Africa which enters the Atlantic to the N of Cape Lopez.
AVONMORE, the most northerly of the three head-waters of the Ovoca in co. Wicklow. It is formed by a confluence of rivulets in the vicinity of Glendalough.

AVOVA (CAPE), a promontory of Asia Minor, on the W side of the gulf of Adalia, in N lat. 36° 35', E long. 30° 38′ 22"; 24 m. SSW of the town of that name. It is a bold cliff of whitish coloured rock. The pine attains dimensions of extraordinary magnitude on the chain of mountains, the ancient Climax, which extends along the shore to the N.-Beaufort.

AVOYELLES, a co. in the state of Louisiana, U. S., on the W. of the Mississippi. It contains a superficies of 1,080 sq. m., and is intersected by the Atchafalaya and Red river. The lower districts are liable to inundation, and are generally unfit for cultivation; but the table-lands to the W are dry and tolerably fertile, producing cotton, Indian corn, potatoes, &c., and maintaining numerous herds of cattle, sheep, and pigs. Pop. in 1830, 3,484; in 1840, whites 3,066, slaves 3,472, free coloured 78, total 6,616.
AVRACHA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, in the sanjak and 32 m. NE of Gallipoli, on the sea of Marmora.

AVRANCHES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Manche. —The arrond. comprises a superficies of 97,346 hect., and contains 9 cantons, viz. Avranches, Brecy, Ducey, Granville, La Haye-Pesnel, St. James, Pontorson, Sartilly, and Ville-Dieu. Pop. in 1831, 110,468; in 1836, 110,821; in 1846, 117,909.—The cant. comprises 16 com., and possessed in 1831 a pop. of 16,462.—The town of A. is situated on the l. bank of the Seez, in N lat. 48° 41' 6", W long. 1° 22', 4 m. above the influx of that river into the sea, and 34 m. SSW of St. Lo. It was formerly strongly fortified; and possesses a cathedral of great antiquity, a communal college, a library, a small theatre, and an hospice, and is the seat of a high court of justice. It has manufactories of lace and blond, salt-refineries, 2 printing establishments, salmon-fisheries, and extensive dairies which afford excellent butter. The principal articles of commerce are grain, cider, and white twine. There are four annual fairs. Pop. in 1789, 3,536; in 1836, 7,690; in 1846, 7,561.—This town, the Abrinca or Abrincatae of the Romans, and the Ingena Abrincatuorum of the middle ages, was taken by the Bretons, and destroyed in 1203. It was restored and fortified by St. Louis; and subsequently fell into the hands of the English, in whose possession it remained until 1450. It was formerly the seat of a bishop. In the vicinity is a cold acidulous chalybeate spring.

AVRE, a river of France, which takes its rise on the southern confines of the dep. of Somme, about 4 m. SE of Roye, and curving towards the NW, passes Moreuil and flows into the river Somme at Amiens after a course of 36 m., of which about 12 are navigable. The Don, which it receives on the l., is its principal affluent. The chief articles of which it affords the means of transit are wood and turf.
AVREMESNIL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Basqueville. Pop. 1,324.

AVRET-HISSAR, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, 20 m. NNW of Salonika, near the l. bank of the Vardar. In the vicinity, on a commanding eminence, are the ruins of the castle of Marulia, which withstood the siege of Ornasberg, in the year of the Hegira 775, for several months, and from which the name of the town, signifying 'The lady's castle,' is derived.
AVRILLE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne-et-Loire, cant. of Angers. Pop. 920.--Also a commune in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Talmont. Pop. 889.
AVROULT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Fauquembergue. Pop. 57.
AVRY-DEVANT-PONT, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the canton and about 8 m. SSW of Eryburg. Pop. 278.

AWA, a fort of Hindostan, 39 m. ENE of Agra. -Also a principality and town of Japan, in the SE of the island of Nifon; bounded on the N by the prin. of Kazousa; on the E and S by the ocean; and on the W by the gulf of Yedo. It is intersected by several ranges of mountains, enclosing valleys and plains of great fertility. Its principal agricultural productions are rice and other species of grain. The coasts abound with oysters and other varieties of fish. The cap. is situated on the S coast, 66 m. ESE of Yedo.—Also a principality and town of Japan, in the island of Selkokf, bounded on the N by the prin. of Sanouki; on the E by the Kino channel; and on the S and W by the prin. of Tosa. It is to a considerable extent mountainous. The level districts afford excellent pasturage. The principal industrial occupations of the inhabitants are the rearing of cattle, and seal and pearl fishing. This prin. comprises 9 provinces. The cap. of the same name is situated on a bay of the S coast, in N lat. 33° 53'. It is large and populous; and its port is the best in the island.
AWAININYOO, a town of Birmah, in Pegu, on the r. bank of the Setang, 36 m. above the town of Setang, and 45 m. NNE of Pegu.
AWAJ, or AWADJ, a river of Syria, which takes its rise to the E of the Jebel-Heish, in the pashalik of Damascus, and running in a generally E direction, terminates in the Bahr-el-Merdj.
AWAL. See BAHREIN.
AWARI, a town of Guinea, in the Yariba territory, to the N of the mountain pass of Chaki, 30 m. SW of Bohoo, and 80 WSW of Eyeo or Katunga. AXH
AWATH (RAS), a headland on the coast of Ajan, in N lat 5° 30'.
AWATSKA. See AVATCHA.
AWBEG, a tributary of the Lee, in co. Cork. It has a course of about 12 m.—Also a tributary of the Blackwater in the same co.

AWE (Loch), a beautiful lake in the district of Lorn, in Argyleshire, between the head of Loch Fyne and Loch Etive. It is 25 m. in length, but throughout the greater part of its extent does not exceed 1 m. in breadth. It discharges its waters by a stream of the same name into Loch Etive.
AWIRS (LES), a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and arrond. of Liege, cant. of Hologne, 8 m. SW of Liege. It possesses extensive alum-works. In the vicinity is the castle of Aigremont. Pop. 1,091.
AWLISCOMBE, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. N by W of Honiton. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 590.

AWRE, a parish in Gloucestershire, 23 m. ENE of Blakeney, on the estuary of the Severn. Area 4,120 acres. Pop. 1,277. The South Wales railway company propose to carry their line across the Severn here by a wooden viaduct 858 yards in length, and 54 ft. above high-water mark, with arches of 100 ft. span. Or by an alternative line through a tunnel under the river 11 m. in length, passing under the river at Hock-Crib, where it is a 1 m. broad.

AWSENOVA, or Avsenova, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Vitebsk, near the r. bank of the Dvina, 23 m. NNW of Dinaburg, and 98 m. SE of Riga. AX. See AxE. AX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Arriege, and arrond. of Foix. The canton comprises 14 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 8,380. The town is situated on the r. bank of the Arriege, 23 m. SSE of Foix. It is celebrated for its thermal springs and baths. It has manufactories of linen and bleacheries, and 3 annual fairs.
AXAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. of Roquefort-de-Sault. It has manufactories of steel. Pop. 546.

AXBRIDGE, a parish and market-town in Somersetshire, 10 m. W of Wells, and 130 W by S of London. Area of p. 540 acres. Pop. in 1831, 928; in 1841, 1,045.—The town is situated under the southern ridge of the Mendip hills. It consists principally of a neat street, running E and W. It was formerly a borough by prescription, and sent members to parliament during the reigns of the first three Edwards. It is now one of the polling-places for the members for East Somersetshire. The Axe drainage, which took place about 50 years ago, improved the value of property in the neighbourhood of A. so much, that land, which before that time was reckoned only worth about 2s. 6d. per acre, is now rented at £5 and £6.
AXE. See Ax.

AXE, a river in Somersetshire, rising in the Mendip hills, about 2 m. from Wells, and falling into the Severn near Uphill, after a course of about 25 m. The Bristol and Exeter railway crosses it near its mouth.—Also a river which rises near Chedington, in Dorset, and entering Devonshire at Ford, falls into the British channel at Axmouth. It has a course of about 20 m., and is navigable for about 4 m. from its mouth.

AXEL, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Zeeland, and arrond. of Hulst, situated in a marshy district, insulated by an arm of the eastern estuary of the Schelde, 7 m. E of Hulst, and 21 WNW of Antwerp. It is a place of considerable strength; and has extensive hydraulic works constructed to facilitate the passage of the canal of Terneusen. Pop. 2,200.
AXFORD, a tything in the p. of Ramsbury, Wiltshire, 3 m. ENE of Marlborough. Pop. 485.

AXHOLME (ISLE OF), an island formed by the rivers Trent on the E, Don on the W, and Idle on the S, in the western division of the wapentake of Manley, Lincolnshire. It is 17 m. in length, and 5 m. in breadth; and contains about 47,000 acres. It comprises the parishes of Althorpe, Belton, Crowle, Epworth, Haxey, Luddington, and Owston, with their respective hamlets; and is crossed by the Stamforth and Keadby canal. At a remote period this district probably formed part of the great forest of the Brigantes; for the trunks of oaks, firs, and other trees, are often found here buried at a small distance from the surface of the ground. The pop. in 1831, including that of West Stockwith, which belongs to Nottinghamshire, was 12,150.

AXEVAL, or AXEWALL, a village of Sweden, in the district of Mariestad, 7 m. E of Skara, and 27 SSW of Mariestad. It is a place of considerable strength; and contains the ruins of an ancient and celebrated fortress of the same name.

AXIM, or FORT St. ANTHONY, a settlement in Upper Guinea, on the Gold coast, between Capes Three Points and Apollonia, situated at the mouth of a small river in N lat. 4° 52', about 3 m. E of the Seenee or Ancobra. This fort, erected by the Portuguese in 1600, and now in the possession of the Dutch, has a commanding appearance, being three stories in height, with walls 4 ft. thick, and embrasures for cannon and loopholes for musketry. It is garrisoned from Elmina; and controls the neighbouring territory, of which the Dutch claim an extent of about 30 m. in length along the coast, and 20 m. into the interior. The principal productions of the soil, which is generally fertile, are rice and pine-apples; but there is difficulty in procuring ship-supplies here, with the exception of wood and water. Gold and ivory are also procured in the district in considerable quantities.
AXIOPOLI. See RASSOVA.

AXMINSTER, a hundred in the division of Honiton, Devonshire, comprising 13 parishes. Area, 51,930 acres. Pop. in 1831, 13,939; in 1841, 14,781.—Also a market-town and parish, in the hund. of Axminster, including the tythings of Abbey, Beerhall, Shepwick, Smallridge, Town, Trill, Uphay, Weycroft, Westwater, and Wyke. Area 6,590 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,719; in 1841, 2,860. The town, which derives its name from its situation on the river Axe, and from a minster founded here by King Athelstan, stands on a rising ground, in an agreeable situation near the confluence of the Axe and Yarty. It is large and irregularly built; but the streets are spacious. The principal manufactures are carpets, broad and narrow cloths, cottons, tapes, druggets, gloves, &c. Of these, the most important is the carpet-manufacture, for which A. is famous, and which was established in 1755.

AXMOUTH, a parish in the hund, and union of Axminster, Devon, on the coast between Lyme and Sidmouth; 3 m. S of Colyton. The river Axe here falls into the English channel. The small bay at its mouth was formerly much resorted to as a harbour for coasting-vessels. Piers have also been erected at the mouth of the Axe, under the shelter of which vessels of 100 tons burden discharge their cargoes. Pop. in 1801, 375; in 1831, 646; in 1841, 645. Acres 4,280. An extraordinary landslip took place here on the 25th of December, 1839, by which a chasm of from 200 to 300 ft. in depth, from 400 to 600 ft. wide, and above 11 m. in length, was formed. AXO. or Asso, a town in the island of Cephalonia, on a pay of the same name on the NW coast.
AXTON, a hundred in the co. of Kent, forming the E part of the lathe of Sutton-et-Hone, and having the old hundreds of Dartford and Wilmington united with it. Area 39,280 acres.

AXUM, a town of Abyssinia, the ancient cap. of AYA the kingdom of Tigre, 120 m. SW of Arkiko on the Red sea, in N lat. 14° 6′ 36″, and E long. 38° 55′. It is situated in a fertile plain, and consists of about 600 houses; but it still retains, in the ruins of temples, palaces, and obelisks, magnificent monuments of its former splendour. One of the obelisks, consisting of a single block of granite, 60 ft. in height, beautifully proportioned and sculptured in bold relief, and terminating in a kind of patera, is in a good state of preservation. Tradition dates its erection in A. D. 300, in the reign of the emperor Æizana. The temples and palaces are of Grecian architecture, and are generally ascribed to the Ptolemies. The principal of the more modern edifices is the church, supposed to have been built in 1637, which, with the exception of that of Chelicut, is the finest in the kingdom. The earliest accredited information which we possess of this town is from Arrian, in the 2d cent. of the Christian era, who mentions, in his Periplus of the Erythræan sea, that it was then the cap. of a powerful kingdom established by the Greeks in Ethiopia, and the entrepot of an extensive commerce, particularly in ivory. Its port was Adula, probably the modern Arkiko. The only manufactures which the modern town possesses are parchment and coarse calicoes.—Bruce.—Salt. AY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, and arrond. of Rheims.-The cant. comprises 19 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 11,710.—The town is finely situated on a rising ground, near the r. bank of the Marne, 15 m S of Rheims. It has a town-house, several public fountains, and some fine promenades, one of which extends across the river to Epernay, a distance of 3 m. Its principal manufactures are hemp, cloth, casks, baskets, articles of turnery, and vinegar. There are also distilleries of brandy. The white wines, for which the adjacent district is celebrated, form the chief article of commerce. Pop. 2,727. AY, a small river of France, in the dep. of La Manche, which, after a short course from S to N, enters the British channel at the harbour of St. Germain.

AY (Pulo), or Pulo WAY, an island in the Asiatic archipelago, in the Banda group, in S lat. 4° 27', E long. 129° 57'. It is 9 m. in circumf., and is very populous. It is fertile, and produces the nutmeg in great luxuriance; but is difficult of access, from the breakers which line its coasts.

AY (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, and cant. of Meung-sur-Loire. It produces good red wines. Pop. 1,122. AY, a fort of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, 6 leagues N of Recife. It defends the mouth of a small river of the same name.
AYA, a mountain of Spain, a summit of the Oyarzun chain, on the NE confines of Guipuscoa, 7 m. S of Irun. Its lofty pyramidal form renders it an important land-mark.
AYA, or AYADA, a river of Spain, in the prov. of Alava, which rises about 5 m. ENE of Parizo, and, running to the W, unites with the Zadorra, 3 m. above the confluence of that river with the Ebro.
AYABACA, a town of Ecuador, in Assuay, 25 m. WSW of Valladolid, and 50 m. SSW of Loxa.
AYA-BARUN. See AIA.
AYACE, a town of Piedmont, 5 m. NNE of Chatillon, and 23 m. ENE of Aosta.

AYACU'CHO, a department of Peru, embracing the basin of the Lower Jauja and of the Rio Apurimac, and subdivided into the prov. of Huancavelica, Lucanas, Tayacaja, Castrovireyna, and Parinacochas on the W slope of the Andes; and those of Guamanga, Guanta, Congallo, Anco, and Andaguilas on the E side of that range. Area 48,000 sq. m. Pop. 240,000. The cap. is Guamanga. A few miles to the ENE of that city is a plain lying to the E of the ridge of Condorcanque, on which the patriot forces under Sucre defeated the royalists under Valdez on the 9th of December, 1824, took the viceroy and his whole force prisoners, and put an end to the Spanish dominion in South America.
AYAGUZ. See AIAGAS.

AYAMONTE, a frontier and fortified town of Spain, in the prov. of Seville, on the l. side of the embouchure of the Guadiana, of which it is the key and fort, 24 m. W of Huelva, and 82 m. WSW of Seville; in N lat. 37° 12'. It is the capital of the partido, and possesses 2 parish-churches, a convent, and some manufactories of silk, soap, and pottery. It has a small port and building-docks, and a considerable fishery of sardines. Pop. 6,350.
AYAPOK. See OYAPOK.
AYAPOTA, a town of Peru, in the province and 105 m. ESE of the town of Cuzco, on one of the head-streams of the Paro or Beni.

AYAR-NOR, a lake of Chinese Tartary, in Soongaria, about 110 m. SSW of the Kesil-Vachi Nor. It is 36 m. in length from E to W, and 21 m. in breadth from N to S. The principal rivers which terminate their course in this lake are the Loklon, Khoutoubi, and Manar.
AYAS. See AIAS.
AYASALOK. See AIASALUK.
AYAVIRI, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. of Puno, 50 m. NW of Asangaro.

AYBAR, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Navarre, on the r. bank of the Aragon, 3 m. SW of Sanguessa, and 25 m. SE of Pampeluna. The battle between John, king of Castile, and his son Don Carlos, in which the latter was defeated, took place here in 1451.

AYCLIFFE (GREAT), a parish in the co. palatine of Durham, 5 m. N of Darlington. It includes the townships of Great Aycliffe, Brafferton, Preston-le- Skerne, and Woodham. Area 10,490 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,372, of whom 823 were in the township of Great A.
AYDAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of St. Amand-Tallende. Pop. 1,649.
AYDIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, and cant. of Garlin, which affords excellent red and white wine known as "vins de Viquebille." Pop. 566.

AYDIUS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of Accous, in the valley of Aspe, and near the r. bank of the Gave, 15 m. S of Oleron, and 25 m. SSW of Pau. It possesses some thermal springs.
AYDON, a township in the parish of Corbridge, Northumberland, 2 m. NE of Corbridge. Pop. 83.
AYELO DE MALFERIT, a village of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, on the river Clarion, in the administrative division of Onteniente. Pop. 2,560. The environs are fertile.

AYEN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, arrond. of Brives.-The cant. comprises 12 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 10,478.—The village lies 13 m. WNW of Brives, and 23 m. W of Tulle. Pop. 974. In the environs are mines of copper and of silver in combination with lead and antimony.
AYER, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, near the r. bank of the Navigenza, 8 m. above the confluence of that river with the Rhone.
AYER AJI, a town of Sumatra, on the W coast, in the vicinity of which is a volcano.
AYER BANGIS, a town of Sumatra, on the W coast. AYL
AYERBE, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 22 m. WNW of Huesca. It is of Gothic origin. Pop. 1,560.
AYHERRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrénées, cant. of La Bastide-Clairence. Pop. 1,513.

AYILA, a chain of mountain table-lands which stretch across the Crimea from E to W. They abound in the interior with picturesque valleys of the richest luxuriance. Seaward they present a series of rugged acclivities which at a distance of from 1 m. to 2 m. from the coast attain a height of 2,000 t. On the north they gradually sink down into the plain. The summit of this table-land is gained from the coast by an ascent partly natural and partly artificial. On some points of the greatest elevation the snow lies till the latter end of May. The name is evidently the Tartar yaila, a name given to highlying pasture-ground.
AYJONES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, on the r. bank of the Guadiana, at the confluence of the Estenilla with that river, 22 m. SE of Guadaloupe, and 68 SW of Toledo.
AYLBURTON, a chapelry in the p. of Lidney, Gloucestershire, 41 m SW of Blackeney. Pop. 468.
AYLESBEARE, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. WSW of Ottery-St.-Mary. Area 3,750 acres. Pop. 982.
AYLESBURY, a hundred in Bucks, near the centre of the county. Area 60,450 acres. Pop. in 1831, 18,413; in 1841, 19,154.

AYLESBURY, or AILESBURY, a borough and parish in the hund. of Aylesbury, co. of Buckingham; 38 m. NW of London by Edgeware; and 18 m. from Buckingham. The town stands in the rich tract of land called the vale of Aylesbury, and was formerly governed by a corporation, under a charter of Mary, dated 1554; but from neglect and non-exercise of privilege, the charter was forfeited in the reign of Elizabeth. Aylesbury district, comprising the hundreds of A., Ashendon, and Cottesloe, and embracing a pop. in 1841 of 56,764, now returns to parliament the two members originally returned by the corporation. A. is also the place of nomination and polling in county-elections. A wool-fair was established here in 1834. Above 800,000 ducks are annually supplied to the London markets from this place. A branch railroad from this town, 7½ m. in length, communicates with the Birmingham railway at the village of Cheddington, 35 m. from London. The line is nearly straight, and was executed at an expense of £57,250. It has been leased to the London and Birmingham railway company, and yields the proprieters about 4 per cent. There is also a branchcanal to this town from the Grand Junction canal. The family of Brudenell Bruce takes the title of marquess from Aylesbury. Pop. of parish and town in 1801, 3,186; in 1831, with Walton, 5,021; in 1841, 5,429. Acres 3,200.
AYLESBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. W of Great Grimsby. Area 2,110 acres. Pop. 201.

AYLESFORD, a lathe or division of Kent, bounded on the N by the Thames; on the W by the lathe of Sutton-at-Hone; on the S by Sussex; and on the E by the lathe of Scray. It includes the hundreds of Hoo, Shamwell, Toltingtrough, Chatham and Gillingham, Wrotham, Larkfield, Littlefield, Twyford, Tunbridge, Washlingestone, Branchley and Horsemonden, Eyehorne and Maidstone. Pop. in 1831, 91,468; in 1841, 103,166. Area 233,580 acres.
AYLESFORD, a parish in the W division of Kent, 31 m. NNW of Maidstone. Area 3,330 acres. Pop. 1,344.

AYLESHAM, a town and parish in Norfolkshire, 13 m. N by W of Norwich. Area of p. 4,250 acres. Pop. 2,448. The town is situated on the Bure, which is navigable from this to Yarmouth. Its principal manufacture is that of hosiery.
AYLESTONE, a parish in Leicestershire, 21 m. S by W of Leicester. Area 3,840 acres. Pop. 757.
AYLLONES, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 48 m. NE of the city of Segovia, on the r. bank of the Grado or Riaza, in the partido of Llerena. Pop. 2,108.

AYLMER (Loch), a sheet of water in Arctic America to the NW of Clinton-Colden lake, intersected by the parallel of 64° 15' N, and the meridian of 109° W. It is about 60 m. in length from SE to NW, and 30 m. in average breadth. It contains several islands, many of them consisting of masses of granite. The surrounding country is characterized by small hills covered with boulders of granite.—Buck.
AYLMERTON, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. WSW of Cromer. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. 289.
AYLSHAM. See AYLESHAM.
AYLTON, a parish in Herefordshire, 4 m. W of Ledbury. Area 810 acres. Pop. 69.
AYMARAEZ. See AIMARAEZ.
AYMARGUE. See AIMARGUES.
AYMERIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, and com. of Berlaimont. Pop. 202.
AYMESTRY, a parish in Herefordshire, 8 m. NW of Leominster, comprising the townships of Conhope and Overlye. Area 7,070 acres. Pop. 958.

AYMOND (Mount), a summit of Patagonia, about 85 m. WNW of Cape Virgins, in S lat. 52° 06' 35", W long. 69° 30' 30". It forms an important land-mark in the navigation of the eastern entrance of the straits of Magalhaens.
AYMORES. See Organs.
AYNA, or AINA, a town of Spain in New Castile, in the prov. of La Mancha, near the confluence of the Madeira and Mondo, in the partido of Yeste, and 30 m. ESE of Alcarez. Pop. 2,387.
AYNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of La-Capelle-Marival, 16 m. NW of Figeac. Pop. 1,445.
AYNAM. See Tunquin.
AYNHO, or Avon-ho, a parish in Northamptonshire, 6 m. WSW of Brackley. Area 2,330 acres. Pop. 662.
AYO. See A10.
AYORA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 50 m. SW of Valencia, in the partido of Jarafuel. It is of Arabic foundation. Pop. 4,524.

AYOS, or Eyos, a powerful and warlike Negro tribe, who inhabit the district of Upper Guinea, near the Kong mountains, extending along the S of Sudan, and NE of the kingdom of Dahomey. They are reputed to be of industrious habits, and to have some manufactures of cotton-fabrics
AYOTLA, a town of Mexico, on the Vera Cruz road, 7 leagues from Mexico.
AYOTT (St. LAWRENCE), a parish of Herefordshire, 2½ m. W of Welwyn. Area 980 acres. Pop. 134.
AYOTT (Sr. Peter), a parish adjoining the above. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. 240.

AYR, a river, which rises in the E extremity of the parish of Muirkirk, in Ayrshire, and after a course of about 33 m. nearly due W, in which it divides the county at its broadest part into two nearly equal portions, falls into the sea at the town of Ayr, where its estuary forms the harbour. It is for some miles of its course only a small rivulet, flowing among holms and haughs through an open moorland district; but, being joined by the Greenock, and 'the haunted Garpal,' it becomes a large body of water; and is augmented by 'the winding Lugar' at Barskimming, and by 'the brawling Coil' at Shaws. Most of its course for the last 20 m. is bounded by steep rocky banks, covered with wood, and in several places highly picturesque.

AYR, a parish and town in Ayrshire. The p. is X++++PX+ about 5 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth, and is bounded on the N by the river just described, which divides it from the p. of Newton-upon-Ayr. The surface is flat and sandy, but interspersed with plantations and villas. Towards the E the country rises gradually; in the immediate neighbourhood of the sea there is a good deal of light shifting sand. Pop. in 1801, 5,492; in 1831, 7,606; in 1841, 8,264. The burgh of Ayr, the county-town of Ayrshire, is 75 m. SW of Edinburgh, 34 m. by road, and 40 m. by railway, from Glasgow, and 12 m. from Kilmarnock. It is situated at the western end of a fertile and beautiful valley, on the S bank of the Ayr, at its influx into the frith of Clyde. The principal or High street is broad and spacious. At the end of this street is 'the Auld brig o' Ayr,' consisting of four lofty and strongly framed arches, said to have been built in the reign of Alexander III., and connecting the town with Newton-on-Ayr; 150 yards below is 'the New brig,' a fine structure of five arches, built in 1787-8, from a design by Robert Adam. The trading importance of A. is chiefly connected with its port, whence coals are extensively exported to Ireland. Pig-iron, coal-tar, freestone, and lime are also extensively exported. The shipping belonging to the port in 1846 amounted to 41 vessels, of a total tonnage of 4,401 tons. The arrivals and sailings in 1846 were 2,366 vessels of 135,393 tons; of these 28 vessels, or 5,915 tons, were either foreign or colonial. The harbour is formed by the mouth of the river, between piers, having the entrance sheltered by a detached breakwater, with an opening 320 ft. wide. The banks of the river are quayed from the bridge seaward, and afford 1,200 yards of wharfage. The position of the N pier according to the Admiralty tables is N lat. 55° 27', W long. 4° 38'; according to Mr. Galbraith, N lat. 55° 28′ 53″, W long. 4° 36′ 21″. Ayr unites with Irvine, Oban, Inverary, and Campbelton, in sending a member to parliament.

AYRSHIRE, a large county on the SW coast of Scotland, which derives its name from the town just described. It is bounded by Renfrewshire on the N and NE; by the counties of Lanark and Dumfries on the E; by the stewartry of Kirkcudbright on the SE; by Wigtonshire on the S; and by Loch Ryan, the North channel, and the frith of Clyde on the W. Its length, from Galloway-burn upon the N side of Loch Ryan, to Kelly-burn which divides it from Renfrewshire, is in a direct line 60 m.; its breadth from E to W is in some places 30 m. Its average length does not, however, exceed 50 m., while its average breadth is about 20 m. It contains, according to Mr. Aiton, whose admeasurements we have now given, 1,600 sq. m.; but, according to Sir John Sinclair's calculations, founded on Arrowsmith's map, only 1,045 sq. m. Ayrshire has nearly the form of a half-moon, concave towards the sea, and convex on the land side. A considerable part of Carrick, and some parts of Kyle and Cunningham towards the inland verges, are hilly; and that part of Ayrshire which borders with the counties of Dumfries and Galloway justly merits the name of mountainous. A large range of A., from the Doon to the N of Ardrossan, is a plain open country, neither level nor hilly, but rising from the shore by an easy acclivity, till it terminates in mountains on the SE, and moorish hills on the E boundaries. The principal elevations are on the southern border of Carrick. On the western skirts of the parish of Muirkirk there are some lofty hills, the most conspicnous of which is Cairntable, which rises to an alt. of 1,650 ft. above sea-level.—The climate is similar to that of other districts situated on the W coast of Britain. For more than two-thirds of the year the wind blows from the SW, and the rains are often copious, and sometimes of long duration.-The principal rivers of Ayrshire are: the Garnock, a small stream, which rises on the borders of Renfrewshire, 10 m. above Kilwinning, flows southward, receives the Lugton, and falls into the harbour of Irvine; the Irvine, which has its source near Loudon-hill, on the confines of Lanarkshire, and thence proceeds westward by Derval, Newmills, Galston, Riccarton, &c., until augmented by many rivulets it flows into the sea at Irvine; the Ayr, already described, which holds a western course nearly parallel to the Irvine; and the Doon, from Loch-Doon, on the N border of Kirkcudbrightshire, which flows NNW to the sea near the mouth of the Ayr. The Girvan, and the Stinchar or Ardstinchar, two inconsiderable streams, issue from small lakes near the border of Kirkcudbrightshire, and flow SW to the sea, into which they fall, the former at Girvan, and the latter at Ballantrae. The principal loch is Loch Doon. There are several small lochs in different quarters of the county. -Clay or argillaceous earth is the most common soil; in different quarters it has been found from 40 to 200 ft. in depth. This species of soil is naturally so tenacious that it can only be ploughed when in a state of moisture; but by summer-fallowing, and the application of lime and other manure, it is convertible into fine rich loam. There is a greater proportion of moss and moor ground than any other in this co. The origin of the extensive mosses of Ayrshire may be traced to the overthrow of the forests which at one time covered great tracts of land in Scotland. The following is the extent of the different kinds of soil in the county, according to Mr. Aiton: Clay soil, Acres. In the district of Carrick, Chalmers assigns to these different classes of soil the following proportions: clay soil 261,960 acres; sandy soil 120,110; moor lands 283,530.—The mineralogy of A. is interesting. The higher parts of Carrick abound in unmixed granite of a greyish colour: breccia, whinstone, greenstone, and red sandstone, are also found in the same district. The coal-district of Scotland, which intersects the island from the Atlantic to the German ocean, runs through the centre of A., from the shore to its inland verges. Copper and lead are both wrought,—the latter to some extent at Daleagles. Mill-stones are quarried near Kilbride; and a species of fire-stone near Auchinleck. Iron-stone is found in different parts of Carrick, and in the higher parts of Kyle. In the parish of Stair, antimony and molybdena have been found; and, in several parts of the county, that species of whetstone known by the name of Water-of-Ayr stone, abounds. Chalybeate springs—some of them strongly impregnated with sulphur-are found in almost every parish. -The size of the Ayrshire improved dairy cows varies from 20 to 40 stones English, according to the quality or abundance of their food. Ten Scots pints per day is not uncommon produce for this breed. Ayrshire is divided into three districts, or bailiages, which, though constantly occurring in history, and in the language of the country at this day, have no longer a separate legal existence: viz., Cunningham, Kyle, and Carrick.—Cunningham, in general a level and agreeable district of a triangular form and declining gradually towards the sea, is divided from Kyle by the Irvine, intersected by the Garnock, and watered by several streams of little note. Towards the confines of Renfrewshire, it rises into an assemblage of hills with intervening valleys; along the sea-coast, and in the southern part of the district, there are tracts of tolerably flat and fertile soil. Its western angle, however, is mountainous, and the coast is rocky. This district comprehends 260 sq. m., [Playfair,] and has several manufacturing towns and villages.—KYLE, the middle district, consisting of about 380 sq. m., [Playfair,] lies between the rivers Doon and Irvine, and is traversed from E to W by the Ayr, which divides it into King's Kyle on the S, and Kyle-Stewart on the N. Towards the confines of Lanark and Dumfries-shire, it is elevated, rugged, and covered with heath; but the midland and maritime tracts are agreeably diversified, wellcultivated, and planted with villages and seats .-

CARRICK, the southern and most romantic district, including that portion of A. which lies to the south of the river Doon, and consisting of 399 sq. m., [Playfair,] is in general mountainous, with some delightful valleys interspersed, and exhibiting fertile declivities inclining towards the sea-coast. The two valleys watered by the Stinchar and the Girvan present much wild and varied scenery. The manufactures of A. are important. The census of 1831 returned 8,000 males, upwards of 20 years of age, as being engaged throughout A. in difchief localities of this manufacture are Kilbirnie and Beith. The number of flax-mills, in 1838, was 3, employing 172 hands. The cotton manufacture has long been increasing, and is now prosecuted on a large scale. Its chief localities are Catrine, Kildery. Extensive iron-works exist at Muirkirk and Glenbuck. The manufacture of wooden snuff-boxes affords occupation to about 120 hands. Trade has been greatly facilitated by the execution of good roads, and by the formation of several railroads,one of which extends from Troon-point to Kilmarnock; another from Kilmarnock to Dalry; another unites Ayr, Irvine, and Dalry; and another will unite Ayr with Ochiltree and Dumfries. The formation of the line of railway betwixt Glasgow, Paisley, Kilmarnock, and Ayr, will doubtless develope the resources of this shire: several extensive coal-fields have been already opened in the immediate neighbourhood of the line in this county. A canal, 31 m. in length, from Glasgow to Ardrossan was projected several years ago; but only about one-third of the length,—viz. from Glasgow to Johnstone—has been executed. A. returns one member to parliament. The parliamentary constituency, in 1839-40, was 4,274; in 1842-3, 4,191. The two boroughs of Ayr and Irvine are associated as contributory burghs with three of the Argyleshire burghs; while Kilmarnock is a contributory burgh of the Renfrew district. The principal towns-to which as separate articles the reader is now generally referred for further information on various points respecting the trade, manufactures, history, and antiquities of this county-are Ardrossan, Ayr, Beith, Girvan, Irvine, Kilwinning, Largs, Maybole, Newton-on-Ayr, Saltcoats, and Stewarton. To A. belong the island of Little Cumbrae, and Ailsa-craig. The number of parishes in A. is 46; of which 16 are in the presbytery of Irvine; 28 in that of Ayr; and 2 in that of Stranraer.—The number of parochial schools in 1834 was 46, under 62 teachers; of schools not parochial 225, under 241 teachers. The total number of scholars 14,800.— The pop. of the county, in 1801, was 84,306; in 1831, 145,100, in 30,501 families, of whom 6,967 families were chiefly employed in agriculture, and 15,193 in trades, handicrafts, and manufactures. In 1841, it amounted to 164,356, of whom 133,404 were natives of the co. The population was thus distributed: The valued rental in 1674, was £191,605. Assessed property, in 1815, £409,983; in 1842-3, £531,318, whereof £390,277 was on lands; £86,429 on houses; and £11,313 on railways. Ayrshire was inhabited in Roman times by the Damnii and the Novantes. After the abdication of the Romans, this district formed a part of the Cumbrian kingdom. During the Saxon heptarchy, Kyle became subject to the kings of Northumbria. The Saxons maintained themselves in A. for many centuries, and have left numerous traces of their presence here. In 1221 the sheriffdom of Ayr was erected. In the wars of Wallace and Bruce, A. was the scene of numerous conflicts with the English; and during the religious persecutions under the last of the Stuarts the men of A. distinguished themselves by their struggles for the maintenance of the rights of conscience.
AYR (COUNTY OF). See MACQUARIE.
AYRAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the r. bank of the Rio Negro, 120 m. above the confluence of that river with the Amazon.

AYRE, a small island in the Mediterranean, of the Balearic group, 1 m. distant from the SE extremity of Minorca. It is 3 m. in length. The greatest depth in mid-channel betwixt this islet and Minorca is only 61 fath.
AYRE. See AIRE.
